name: contributing
context: How to contribute to Dojo — authoring nodes, project structure, node hierarchy, validation checklist, and submission workflow
info: The contributor guide for the Dojo project. Covers the full directory layout (nodes, server, sdk, web, schema), the five-type node hierarchy (ecosystem, standard, skill, context, sub) with nesting rules, how to author a node.json manifest with required and optional fields, URI conventions, discovery fields (aliases, triggers), knowledge-layer authoring (body, sections, links, related, wiki-links), script and schema contracts for executable nodes, dependency declarations, minimal templates for each type, and the validation checklist to run before submitting.
aliases: how to create a dojo node

body:
# Contributing to Dojo

Dojo is a universal knowledge layer for AI agents — a registry of discoverable, composable, hierarchical nodes that agents query to learn and execute. Contributing means adding or improving nodes in the `nodes/` directory, which is the source of truth the server reads on startup and indexes into an in-memory store.

## Project Layout

```
dojo/
├── nodes/     Canonical node tree — all ecosystems and children
├── schema/    JSON Schema for validating node.json manifests
├── server/    Express registry server (REST API)
├── sdk/       JavaScript SDK for discovery and execution
├── web/       Vite + React browser UI
├── skills/    Bundled agent-facing skill packages
├── tests/     Server unit and integration tests
└── docker/    Dockerfile and docker-compose setup
```

## Contribution Flow

1. Pick the right node type based on what it does
2. Create a directory under `nodes/` with a `node.json` manifest
3. Fill in required fields, then discovery and knowledge fields
4. Add scripts and schema for executable nodes
5. Validate against the schema and confirm the server loads it
6. Open a PR with `gh pr create --fill`

See [[dojo/contributing#quickstart]] for the step-by-step and [[dojo/contributing#checklist]] before submitting.

sections.title: Quickstart
sections.body:
1. **Pick the right type:** `skill` or `sub` if it has executable scripts, `standard` if it defines a protocol, `context` if it is info-only.
2. **Create the directory:** add `nodes/<ecosystem>/<name>/node.json`. The directory name matches the node `name`.
3. **Fill required fields:** `name`, `version` (semver), `uri` (matching directory path), `type`, `context` (one-line), `info` (detailed paragraph), `tags`, `parent` (null for ecosystems).
4. **Add discovery fields:** `aliases` (phrases people actually say) and `triggers` (natural-language activation phrases).
5. **Add knowledge fields:** `body` for long-form content, `sections` for addressable chunks, `links` for outgoing references, `related` for semantic edges.
6. **For skills:** add `scripts` with entry points, `schema` for input/output, and `depends` for dependencies.
7. **Validate:** run `cd nodes/dojo && node --test validate/tests/validate-node.test.js` or start the server and check your node loads.
8. **Submit:** commit on a branch and open a pull request with `gh pr create --fill`.
9. Confirm the checklist at [[dojo/contributing#checklist]] passes.

sections.title: Node hierarchy and nesting rules
sections.body:
Five node types form a tree:

- **ecosystem** — broad domain, no parent, top of the tree (e.g. openai, github, docker, aws)
- **standard** — protocol, spec, or convention; can live under ecosystem, skill, or another standard (e.g. rest, graphql, oauth2)
- **skill** — concrete executable capability with scripts; can live under ecosystem, standard, or another skill (e.g. query, deploy, send)
- **context** — info-only, wiki-like knowledge with no scripts; can live anywhere in the tree (e.g. indexing-guide, models, rate-limits)
- **sub** — specialized variant of a skill; must have a skill parent (e.g. deploy-preview, query-explain)

Nesting rules: ecosystems have no parent. Standards can have any parent except `sub`. Skills can have any parent except `sub`. Context nodes can have any parent. Sub nodes must have a skill parent. Only `skill` and `sub` carry `scripts`. Any node can declare `depends` references.

sections.title: Manifest fields reference
sections.body:
**Required:** `name` (unique within parent), `version` (semver), `uri` (slash-separated path matching directory), `type`, `context` (one-line — agents read this first), `info` (detailed paragraph), `tags`, `parent` (null for ecosystems).

**Discovery:** `aliases` (real phrases users say), `triggers` (natural-language activation phrases).

**Knowledge:** `body` (long-form markdown with `[[uri]]` wiki-links and `{{uri}}` inline embeds), `sections` (addressable chunks with `id`, `title`, `body`, `tags`), `links` (outgoing references with context), `related` (semantic relationships: similar, evolution, equivalent, prerequisite, alternative, implements, extends, see-also), `frontmatter` (metadata: created, updated, author, audience, status, confidence).

**Execution (skill/sub only):** `scripts` (array of entry-point or inline scripts with lang, runtime, env, packages), `schema` (JSON Schema for input/output), `depends` (references to other nodes).

See [[dojo/spec#manifest-core]] for the formal definition.

sections.title: Knowledge authoring
sections.body:
Use `body` for the long-form explanation. Use `sections` for addressable chunks — each has an `id`, `title`, `body`, and `tags`. Section order matters because the learn endpoint uses it to build a default reading path.

Use `links` for explicit outgoing references with context annotations. Use `related` for semantic relationships (similarity, prerequisite, alternative, etc.).

Wiki-link syntax: `[[uri]]` links to another node, `[[uri#section-id]]` links to a specific section, `{{uri}}` inlines another node's context one-liner.

A strong knowledge node starts with a short `context`, expands in `info`, teaches in `body`, slices into `sections`, points forward with `links`, and gives the reader at least one executable next step. See [[dojo/knowledge#authoring-patterns]] for quality patterns.

sections.title: Scripts and schema
sections.body:
Scripts make `skill` and `sub` nodes executable. Each script has `id`, `name`, `description`, `lang` (javascript, bash, python), `runtime` (e.g. node>=18), and either `entry` (file path like `./scripts/run.js`) or `inline` (short command). Scripts can declare `env` vars (with `required`, `secret`, `description`) and `packages` (npm dependencies).

Scripts receive input as JSON on stdin and return output as JSON on stdout. Exit 0 for success, exit 1 for failure with stderr for error messages.

Define `schema.input` and `schema.output` as JSON Schema objects so agents know the contract. Prefer `entry` files once the logic is large enough to deserve comments, tests, or reuse.

sections.title: Minimal node templates
sections.body:
**Ecosystem:** `{ name, version, uri, type: "ecosystem", context, info, parent: null, tags, author, license }`

**Skill:** same core plus `type: "skill"`, `parent`, and a `scripts` array with at least one entry defining `id`, `name`, `description`, `lang`, `runtime`, and `entry`.

**Context:** same core plus `type: "context"`, `parent`, and `body` with wiki-links to executable next steps.

**Sub:** same core plus `type: "sub"`, `parent` pointing to a skill URI, and `scripts`.

See the CONTRIBUTING.md file in the project root for full JSON examples of each template.

sections.title: URI format
sections.body:
URIs are slash-separated paths matching the directory structure under `nodes/`. The type is not encoded in the URI — it lives in the manifest. A node at `nodes/openai/chat/node.json` has URI `openai/chat`. The URI must match the node's actual location in `nodes/`.

Examples: `openai` (ecosystem), `openai/chat` (skill), `openai/chat/stream` (sub), `openai/models` (context), `docker/compose/profiles` (standard under a skill).

sections.title: Submission checklist
sections.body:
Before submitting, verify:
- [ ] `node.json` has all required fields (`name`, `version`, `uri`, `type`, `context`, `info`, `tags`)
- [ ] `uri` matches the directory path under `nodes/`
- [ ] `parent` matches the actual parent node's URI (null for ecosystems)
- [ ] `version` is valid semver
- [ ] `type` is one of: `ecosystem`, `standard`, `skill`, `context`, `sub`
- [ ] Scripts only exist on `skill` or `sub` nodes
- [ ] `sub` nodes have a `skill` parent
- [ ] Knowledge fields (`body`, `sections`, `links`) use valid `[[uri]]` wiki-link syntax
- [ ] Validates against `schema/node.schema.json`
- [ ] Server loads your node without errors (`npm run dev` and check logs)
- [ ] For executable skills: scripts run, env vars are documented, input/output schema is defined

sections.title: GitHub pull request flow
sections.body: Create a focused branch, commit the manifest changes, push, and open a pull request with `gh pr create --fill`. Keep the PR description and diff focused so review is about manifest quality, not unrelated churn. Include validation results and explain why the node belongs where it does. Use `gh pr edit` to update the summary if needed.
